expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of the cytoskeleton as a dynamic, multifunctional protein network that maintains cell shape, enables cell motility, facilitates intracellular transport, drives cell division, and transduces mechanical signals, detailing the structure, assembly, regulation, and molecular machinery of microfilaments, intermediate filaments, and microtubules.

core_concept.principle: The cytoskeleton is a dynamic, three-dimensional network of protein filaments (microfilaments, intermediate filaments, and microtubules) that provides structural support, enables movement, and organizes cellular contents through coordinated polymerization, depolymerization, and motor protein activity.
core_concept.explanation: Unlike a static skeleton, the cytoskeleton is highly dynamic, constantly remodeling in response to cellular signals. Microfilaments (actin) generate force for cell crawling and cytokinesis; intermediate filaments provide tensile strength and mechanical integrity; microtubules serve as tracks for intracellular transport and form the mitotic spindle. Motor proteins (myosin, kinesin, dynein) convert chemical energy from ATP hydrolysis into mechanical work, moving cargo along filaments or sliding filaments past each other. The cytoskeleton integrates mechanical and chemical signaling, linking the extracellular matrix to the nucleus via the LINC complex, and regulating gene expression through mechanotransduction. Defects in cytoskeletal components underlie diverse diseases including neurodegenerative disorders (tauopathies), ciliopathies, muscular dystrophies, and cancer metastasis.

image.alt: Fluorescence microscopy composite showing all three cytoskeletal filament types in a fibroblast
image.caption: Triple-stained fibroblast: Microfilaments (actin, red) concentrated at cell periphery and stress fibers; Microtubules (tubulin, green) radiating from centrosome; Intermediate filaments (vimentin, blue) forming a cage around the nucleus. Shows spatial organization and integration of the three systems.

sections.introduction: The cytoskeleton was once thought to be a static framework maintaining cell shape, but is now understood as a dynamic, adaptive system essential for nearly all cellular processes. It comprises three distinct filament systems differing in diameter, subunit composition, dynamics, and function. Microfilaments (7 nm) are the thinnest, composed of actin, and drive cell motility and muscle contraction. Intermediate filaments (10 nm) provide mechanical strength and tissue integrity. Microtubules (25 nm) are the thickest, composed of tubulin, and serve as highways for intracellular transport and chromosomes segregation. These systems do not operate in isolation; they are cross-linked by accessory proteins (spectrin, filamin, MAPs) and coordinated by signaling pathways (Rho GTPases) to respond to environmental cues. The cytoskeleton also functions as a mechanosensor, converting physical forces into biochemical signals that regulate gene expression, differentiation, and survival. Understanding cytoskeletal dynamics is crucial for comprehending development, immunity, neuroscience, and disease mechanisms ranging from metastasis to neurodegeneration.

introduction_image.alt: Evolutionary conservation of cytoskeletal proteins from bacteria to humans
introduction_image.caption: Homologs of actin (MreB), tubulin (FtsZ), and intermediate filaments (crescentin) exist in bacteria, indicating ancient origin. Eukaryotic cytoskeleton expanded in complexity with multicellularity, enabling specialized cell shapes and movements.

key_concept.definition: The cytoskeleton is a dynamic network of protein filaments (microfilaments, intermediate filaments, microtubules) and associated motor proteins that maintains cell shape, enables motility, facilitates intracellular transport, and organizes cell division.
key_concept.context: The term 'skeleton' is misleading; the cytoskeleton is highly dynamic, with filaments constantly polymerizing and depolymerizing (treadmilling, dynamic instability) to adapt to cellular needs. This dynamism requires energy (ATP/GTP hydrolysis) and is tightly regulated by signaling pathways.

image.alt: Time-lapse sequence showing cytoskeletal remodeling during cell migration
image.caption: Sequential images (0, 5, 10 min) show actin polymerization at leading edge (lamellipodia), microtubule extension toward front, and rear retraction. Demonstrates continuous remodeling during movement.

sections.key_concept: The cytoskeleton comprises three filament systems with distinct properties: microfilaments (actin) for force generation, intermediate filaments for mechanical strength, and microtubules (tubulin) for transport and division.

classifications.value: Thinnest filaments (7 nm diameter); composed of actin subunits; involved in cell motility, muscle contraction, cytokinesis, and cell cortex structure
characteristics: Polarized structure: plus end (barbed, fast-growing) and minus end (pointed, slow-growing)
Subunits: Globular actin (G-actin) polymerizes into Filamentous actin (F-actin)
Nucleotide binding: Each actin binds ATP; hydrolysis to ADP occurs after polymerization, affecting stability
Associated proteins: Myosin motors, profilin (promotes assembly), cofilin (promotes disassembly), capZ (caps plus end)
Drug sensitivity: Cytochalasin D (caps plus end, prevents assembly); Phalloidin (stabilizes, prevents disassembly)
classifications.behavior: Actin filaments exhibit treadmilling: subunits add at plus end while dissociating at minus end, maintaining constant length while flowing. In cell migration, actin polymerization at the leading edge pushes the membrane forward (lamellipodia). In muscle, actin slides past myosin filaments via cross-bridge cycling. Actin networks are cross-linked into bundles (filopodia) or gels (cortex) by accessory proteins.

examples.explanation: G-actin-ATP binds to filament plus end. ATP hydrolyzes to ADP-Pi, then Pi releases, leaving ADP-actin. ADP-actin at minus end has lower affinity, dissociating. At steady state, addition rate at plus end equals loss rate at minus end. Critical concentration ($C_c$) differs for each end: $C_c^+ < C_c^-$. Treadmilling occurs when [G-actin] is between $C_c^+$ and $C_c^-$. This flow generates force for protrusion.
examples.equation: $\text{Rate}_{\text{net}} = k_{\text{on}}[\text{G-actin}] - k_{\text{off}}$; Treadmilling when $k_{\text{on}}^+[\text{G-actin}] = k_{\text{off}}^+$ and $k_{\text{on}}^- [\text{G-actin}] < k_{\text{off}}^-$

image.alt: Diagram of actin filament treadmilling
image.caption: Plus end (right): ATP-actin adds. Minus end (left): ADP-actin dissociates. Filament appears to move forward while maintaining length. Energy from ATP hydrolysis drives directionality.

examples.explanation: In sarcomeres, actin thin filaments slide past myosin thick filaments. Myosin heads bind actin, undergo power stroke (pulling actin), release ADP+Pi, bind ATP to detach, then hydrolyze ATP to reset. Z-discs move closer, shortening sarcomere. Ca²⁺ binding to troponin moves tropomyosin, exposing myosin-binding sites on actin. This cycle repeats rapidly during contraction.

image.alt: Sarcomere structure and sliding filament mechanism
image.caption: Relaxed: Tropomyosin blocks myosin sites. Contracted: Ca²⁺ exposes sites, myosin heads pull actin toward M-line. Z-discs converge, sarcomere shortens.

classifications.value: Intermediate diameter (10 nm); composed of tissue-specific proteins (keratins, vimentin, lamins, neurofilaments); provide tensile strength and mechanical integrity
characteristics: Non-polar: Subunits assemble into antiparallel tetramers, forming symmetric filaments without plus/minus ends
Subunits: Fibrous proteins with central α-helical rod domain, flanked by variable head/tail domains
No nucleotide binding: Assembly does not require ATP/GTP hydrolysis; more stable than actin/microtubules
No motor proteins: IFs do not serve as tracks for motors; cargo transport occurs on microtubules/actin
Tissue specificity: Keratins (epithelia), Vimentin (mesenchyme), Neurofilaments (neurons), Lamins (nuclear envelope)
classifications.behavior: IFs form a ropelike network resisting mechanical stress. They anchor to desmosomes (cell-cell) and hemidesmosomes (cell-matrix), distributing tension across tissues. Nuclear lamins line the inner nuclear membrane, providing structural support and organizing chromatin. IFs are highly stable but can be phosphorylated to disassemble during mitosis (e.g., lamins) or stress. Mutations cause fragile tissue diseases (epidermolysis bullosa, ALS).

examples.explanation: Keratin IFs form a cytoplasmic network anchored to desmosomes at cell-cell junctions. This creates a continuous tensile scaffold across an epithelial sheet, distributing mechanical stress. When one cell is stretched, force is transmitted to neighbors via keratin-desmosome connections, preventing tissue rupture. Mutations in keratin genes (K5, K14) cause epidermolysis bullosa simplex: skin blisters upon minor friction due to cytoskeletal collapse.

image.alt: Keratin filaments anchored to desmosomes between epithelial cells
image.caption: Keratin IFs (yellow) connect to desmosomal plaques (red) at cell boundaries. Network spans cytoplasm, linking cells into a mechanically integrated sheet.

examples.explanation: Lamins (A, B, C) form a meshwork beneath the inner nuclear membrane. At prophase, Cdk1-cyclin B phosphorylates lamins, causing depolymerization and nuclear envelope breakdown (NEBD). This allows spindle microtubules to access chromosomes. At telophase, phosphatases dephosphorylate lamins, triggering reassembly around daughter nuclei. This reversible disassembly is unique among IFs.

image.alt: Nuclear lamina disassembly and reassembly during mitosis
image.caption: Interphase: Intact lamina. Prophase: Phosphorylation → depolymerization. Telophase: Dephosphorylation → repolymerization. Regulates nuclear integrity and cell cycle progression.

classifications.value: Thickest filaments (25 nm diameter); composed of α/β-tubulin heterodimers; involved in intracellular transport, cell division, and cilia/flagella structure
characteristics: Polarized structure: plus end (fast-growing, dynamic) and minus end (slow-growing, often anchored at centrosome)
Subunits: α/β-tubulin heterodimers assemble into protofilaments (typically 13) forming hollow tubes
Nucleotide binding: β-tubulin binds GTP; hydrolysis to GDP occurs after polymerization, creating instability
Associated proteins: Kinesin (plus-end directed), Dynein (minus-end directed), MAPs (stabilize), +TIPs (track plus ends)
Drug sensitivity: Taxol (stabilizes, prevents disassembly); Colchicine/Vinblastine (prevents assembly)
classifications.behavior: Microtubules exhibit dynamic instability: stochastic switching between growth (rescue) and shrinkage (catastrophe) phases at plus ends. GTP-tubulin adds to plus end, forming a 'GTP cap' that stabilizes the filament. If hydrolysis outpaces addition, GTP cap is lost, GDP-tubulin lattice becomes unstable, and rapid depolymerization occurs. This allows microtubules to rapidly search cellular space (e.g., capturing chromosomes during mitosis).

examples.explanation: During mitosis, spindle microtubules grow and shrink dynamically from centrosomes. When a plus end encounters a kinetochore on a chromosome, it is stabilized (rescue), forming a kinetochore microtubule. Unattached microtubules undergo catastrophe and shrink. This 'search and capture' mechanism ensures all chromosomes are properly attached before anaphase. The spindle assembly checkpoint monitors attachment, preventing segregation errors.

image.alt: Microtubule dynamic instability during chromosome capture
image.caption: Microtubules grow (green) and shrink (red) dynamically. Kinetochore attachment (yellow) stabilizes plus end. Unattached microtubules undergo catastrophe. Ensures proper chromosome alignment.

examples.explanation: Neurons have long axons (up to 1 meter) requiring transport of organelles, vesicles, and proteins. Microtubules are oriented with plus ends distal (toward synapse). Kinesin motors carry cargo anterograde (cell body → synapse); Dynein carries cargo retrograde (synapse → cell body). Disruption causes neurodegeneration: accumulated waste products (e.g., tau aggregates in Alzheimer's) impair transport, leading to synaptic failure and cell death.

image.alt: Bidirectional axonal transport along microtubules
image.caption: Microtubules (green) oriented plus-end distal. Kinesin (blue) moves cargo anterograde. Dynein (red) moves cargo retrograde. Essential for neuronal survival and function.

principles.principle: Nucleotide Hydrolysis Drives Filament Polarity and Dynamics
principles.statement: ATP/GTP binding and hydrolysis by cytoskeletal subunits create structural asymmetry (polarity) and energetic instability that drive directional assembly, disassembly, and motor protein movement.
principles.application: Actin binds ATP; tubulin binds GTP. Hydrolysis occurs after polymerization, creating ADP/GDP cores less stable than ATP/GTP caps. This energy difference enables treadmilling (actin) and dynamic instability (microtubules), allowing filaments to remodel rapidly without complete disassembly. Motor proteins also hydrolyze ATP to undergo conformational changes for stepping.
principles.example: Microtubule dynamic instability: GTP-tubulin adds to plus end, forming stabilizing cap. If growth slows, hydrolysis catches up, cap is lost, GDP-tubulin lattice curls and peels apart (catastrophe). This allows rapid reorganization during cell division.
principles.analogy: Nucleotide hydrolysis is like a timer on a building block: fresh blocks (ATP/GTP) stick together strongly; old blocks (ADP/GDP) become loose and fall off. This allows the structure to grow at one end while shrinking at the other, creating flow without moving the whole structure.

image.alt: Comparison of ATP/GTP hydrolysis effects on actin and microtubule stability
image.caption: Actin: ATP-cap stabilizes plus end; ADP-core destabilizes minus end → treadmilling. Microtubules: GTP-cap stabilizes; GDP-core unstable → dynamic instability. Energy from hydrolysis drives both.

principles.principle: Motor Proteins Convert Chemical Energy into Mechanical Work
principles.statement: Cytoskeletal motor proteins (myosin, kinesin, dynein) use ATP hydrolysis to undergo conformational changes that generate force and movement along filament tracks.
principles.application: Motors have conserved catalytic domains (heads) that bind filaments and ATP, and stalks/tails that bind cargo. The ATPase cycle (bind ATP → hydrolyze → release Pi → release ADP) drives a 'power stroke' moving the motor ~8-10 nm per step. Directionality is determined by motor structure: kinesin moves to plus ends, dynein to minus ends, myosin toward actin plus ends.
principles.example: Kinesin-1 walks hand-over-hand along microtubules: one head bound, other swings forward. ATP binding to trailing head triggers detachment; hydrolysis triggers power stroke. Processivity allows hundreds of steps without detaching, essential for long-distance transport.
principles.analogy: Motor proteins are like molecular walkers: feet (heads) step along a rope (filament), hands (tails) carry a box (cargo). Each step costs a battery (ATP). Different walkers move in different directions (kinesin forward, dynein backward) on the same rope.

image.alt: ATPase cycle and stepping mechanism of kinesin
image.caption: Kinesin heads (blue) bind microtubule (green). ATP binding (1) → hydrolysis (2) → power stroke (3) → ADP release (4). Hand-over-hand motion moves cargo toward plus end.

principles.principle: Accessory Proteins Regulate Filament Assembly and Organization
principles.statement: Hundreds of accessory proteins (nucleators, capping proteins, cross-linkers, severing enzymes) control where, when, and how cytoskeletal filaments assemble, disassemble, and organize into higher-order structures.
principles.application: Nucleators (Arp2/3, formins, γ-TuRC) initiate filament growth. Capping proteins (CapZ) block ends to control length. Cross-linkers (filamin, spectrin) organize filaments into networks or bundles. Severing proteins (cofilin, katanin) cut filaments to create new ends for remodeling. This regulation allows spatial precision (e.g., actin only at leading edge).
principles.example: Arp2/3 complex nucleates branched actin networks at cell leading edge, activated by WASP/WAVE proteins downstream of Rac/Cdc42. This creates lamellipodia for migration. Without Arp2/3, cells cannot form broad protrusions efficiently.
principles.analogy: Accessory proteins are like construction crew: architects (nucleators) start building; foremen (capping proteins) stop building at right height; scaffolders (cross-linkers) connect beams; demolition crew (severing proteins) tear down old structures. Together they build dynamic structures on demand.

image.alt: Roles of accessory proteins in actin filament regulation
image.caption: Nucleators (Arp2/3) start filaments. Cappers (CapZ) stop growth. Cross-linkers (filamin) form networks. Severers (cofilin) cut filaments. Integrated regulation creates specific structures (lamellipodia, stress fibers).

sections.section_title: Key Components and Motor Protein Categories

components.definition: Cytoskeletal function requires filament subunits (actin, tubulin, IF proteins), motor proteins (myosin, kinesin, dynein), and regulatory accessory proteins that coordinate assembly and activity.
components.equation: Motor velocity: $v = d \times k_{\text{step}}$, where $d$ is step size (~8 nm) and $k_{\text{step}}$ is stepping rate (ATPase turnover)
components.interpretation: Motor velocity depends on step size (fixed by filament geometry) and ATPase rate (regulated by load, cargo, signaling). Kinesin-1: ~800 nm/s; Dynein: ~1000 nm/s; Myosin II: ~5000 nm/s (muscle). Load slows motors (force-velocity relationship), ensuring cargo is not dropped under high resistance.

image.alt: Structural domains of myosin, kinesin, and dynein motor proteins
image.caption: Myosin (left): Head (motor), neck (lever), tail (cargo). Kinesin (center): Heads (motor), stalk (dimerization), tail (cargo). Dynein (right): Ring-shaped AAA+ motor domain, stalk, tail. All convert ATP to motion.

categories.description: Myosin superfamily of actin-based motors; myosin II drives muscle contraction and cytokinesis; myosin V transports cargo; myosin I links membrane to actin.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Myosin heads bind actin and ATP. ATP hydrolysis cocks the lever arm; Pi release triggers power stroke, sliding actin. ADP release resets cycle. Myosin II forms bipolar filaments (thick filaments) pulling actin from both sides. Myosin V is processive (two heads coordinate), walking long distances without detaching. Regulation via light chain phosphorylation (muscle) or Ca²⁺/calmodulin (non-muscle).

examples.explanation: During cell division, actin and myosin II assemble into a contractile ring at the cell equator. Myosin II filaments slide actin filaments past each other, constricting the ring like a drawstring. RhoA GTPase activates formin (actin nucleation) and ROCK (myosin light chain kinase), promoting assembly and activation. Ring constriction cleaves the cell into two daughters. Failure causes binucleate cells.

image.alt: Actin-myosin contractile ring during cytokinesis
image.caption: Actin (red) and myosin II (blue) form ring at equator. Myosin slides actin, constricting ring. Cleavage furrow ingresses, separating daughter cells. Regulated by RhoA signaling.

examples.explanation: Myosin V transports vesicles, mitochondria, and mRNA along actin filaments, especially in cell periphery where microtubules are sparse. Two heads coordinate for processivity: one head always bound. Cargo binding activates motor autoinhibition. Mutations cause Griscelli syndrome: defects in melanosome transport lead to hypopigmentation and immune defects.

image.alt: Myosin V walking along actin filament with cargo
image.caption: Myosin V (blue) carries vesicle (yellow) along actin (red). Hand-over-hand stepping ensures continuous transport. Important for peripheral distribution where microtubules do not reach.

categories.description: Kinesins (mostly plus-end directed) and dyneins (minus-end directed) transport cargo along microtubules; essential for axonal transport, mitosis, and cilia function.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Kinesin-1: Two heads coordinate for processive walking toward plus ends. Dynein: Large multi-subunit complex; AAA+ ring domain generates force; moves toward minus ends. Dynactin complex enhances dynein processivity and cargo binding. Regulation via phosphorylation, cargo adaptors (JIP, BICD2), and autoinhibition. Opposing motors on same cargo create tension, regulated by signaling.

examples.process: Mitotic Spindle Assembly and Chromosome Segregation
examples.explanation: Kinesins and dynein organize spindle microtubules. Kinesin-5 (Eg5) is plus-end directed, sliding antiparallel microtubules apart to separate spindle poles. Kinesin-13 (MCAK) depolymerizes microtubule ends, regulating dynamics. Dynein pulls on astral microtubules to position spindle. During anaphase, kinesins slide overlapping microtubules, elongating spindle. Errors cause aneuploidy.

image.alt: Motor proteins organizing mitotic spindle
image.caption: Kinesin-5 (blue) pushes poles apart. Dynein (red) pulls on astral microtubules. Kinesin-13 (green) depolymerizes ends. Coordinated action ensures proper chromosome segregation.

examples.explanation: Cilia are assembled and maintained by IFT: kinesin-2 carries building blocks (tubulin) to ciliary tip (anterograde); dynein-2 carries turnover products back (retrograde). IFT particles (complexes A and B) link motors to cargo. Defects cause ciliopathies (polycystic kidney disease, Bardet-Biedl syndrome) due to failed cilia assembly or signaling.

image.alt: IFT particle movement along ciliary axoneme
image.caption: Kinesin-2 (blue) moves IFT particles (yellow) to tip. Dynein-2 (red) returns particles. Tubulin added at tip. Continuous turnover maintains cilia structure and function.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Calculating Critical Concentration and Treadmilling

worked_examples.title: Determining Actin Polymerization Dynamics and Critical Concentration
worked_examples.problem: For actin filaments, the critical concentration at the plus end is $C_c^+ = 0.1 \ \mu\text{M}$ and at the minus end is $C_c^- = 0.8 \ \mu\text{M}$. The on-rate constant is $k_{\text{on}} = 10 \ \mu\text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ for both ends, and off-rate constant is $k_{\text{off}} = 1 \ \text{s}^{-1}$ (plus) and $8 \ \text{s}^{-1}$ (minus). (a) Calculate the net growth rate at each end when [G-actin] = $0.5 \ \mu\text{M}$. (b) Does treadmilling occur? (c) What [G-actin] is required for steady-state treadmilling?

step_by_step.explanation: Recall net growth rate equation: $\text{Rate} = k_{\text{on}}[\text{G-actin}] - k_{\text{off}}$. Positive rate = growth; negative = shrinkage.
Determine if treadmilling occurs. Treadmilling requires plus end growth and minus end shrinkage simultaneously.
step_by_step.calculation: Plus end: +4 (growing); Minus end: -3 (shrinking). Yes, treadmilling occurs. Net filament length change = $+4 - 3 = +1 \ \text{subunit/s}$ (slow growth).

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate [G-actin] for steady-state treadmilling (net length change = 0, so $\text{Rate}^+ = -\text{Rate}^-$). Actually, steady state means $\text{Rate}^+ + \text{Rate}^- = 0$, or more precisely, addition at plus equals loss at minus.
step_by_step.calculation: At steady state, $[\text{G-actin}]$ is between $C_c^+$ and $C_c^-$. Exact treadmilling concentration: $C_{\text{treadmill}} = \frac{k_{\text{off}}^+ + k_{\text{off}}^-}{k_{\text{on}}^+ + k_{\text{on}}^-} = \frac{1 + 8}{10 + 10} = \frac{9}{20} = 0.45 \ \mu\text{M}$. At this concentration, plus end grows at $10(0.45)-1 = 3.5$, minus shrinks at $10(0.45)-8 = -3.5$. Net length constant.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Plus end: +4 subunits/s (growth); Minus end: -3 subunits/s (shrinkage). (b) Yes, treadmilling occurs. (c) Steady-state treadmilling at [G-actin] = 0.45 μM. This demonstrates how intermediate monomer concentrations drive directional flow without net filament growth, essential for cell motility.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Critical concentration ($C_c = k_{\text{off}}/k_{\text{on}}$) determines polymerization threshold. Polarity creates different $C_c$ values at each end. When [monomer] is between $C_c^+$ and $C_c^-$, treadmilling occurs. This principle underlies actin-based cell protrusion and microtubule dynamic instability.

sections.role: Mechanical Support, Motility, Transport, and Division

components.composition: Dense actin meshwork beneath plasma membrane, cross-linked by spectrin, filamin, and myosin II
components.function: Maintains cell shape, resists deformation, generates tension for blebbing and cytokinesis
components.key_feature: Actin-membrane linkers (ezrin/radixin/moesin) connect cortex to integral membrane proteins, transmitting forces

image.alt: Electron micrograph of cell cortex beneath plasma membrane
image.caption: Actin filaments (red) form dense meshwork beneath membrane (yellow). Linker proteins (blue) connect actin to membrane. Provides mechanical integrity and shape.

components.composition: Bundles of actin filaments and myosin II, anchored to focal adhesions at both ends
components.function: Generate contractile force, maintain cell tension, sense substrate stiffness
components.key_feature: RhoA signaling promotes assembly; tension reinforces adhesions via mechanotransduction (vinculin, talin)

image.alt: Fluorescence image of stress fibers in fibroblast
image.caption: Actin (red) and myosin (green) form contractile bundles spanning cell. Anchored at focal adhesions (blue). Generate tension for migration and mechanosensing.

components.composition: Microtubules radiating from centrosomes, captured by kinetochores, organized by motors
components.function: Segregate chromosomes during cell division; position cleavage plane
components.key_feature: Dynamic instability allows chromosome capture; motor proteins organize poles and separate chromosomes

image.alt: 3D reconstruction of mitotic spindle
image.caption: Microtubules (green) from centrosomes (blue) attach to kinetochores (red) on chromosomes (yellow). Motors organize structure for accurate segregation.

function.operation: Cytoskeleton integrates mechanical and chemical signals: actin generates force for motility; microtubules transport cargo and organize division; intermediate filaments provide tensile strength. Coordination via Rho GTPases ensures spatial and temporal precision.
function.energy: ATP/GTP hydrolysis drives filament dynamics and motor movement. Actin treadmilling: ~1 ATP/subunit. Microtubule dynamics: ~1 GTP/dimer. Motor stepping: ~1 ATP/step. High energy cost enables rapid remodeling.
function.mechanism: Nucleotide hydrolysis creates polarity and instability; motor proteins convert chemical to mechanical energy; accessory proteins regulate assembly; signaling pathways (Rho, Rac, Cdc42) coordinate spatial organization.

image.alt: Integrated view of cytoskeletal systems in a migrating cell
image.caption: Leading edge: Actin polymerization (red) pushes membrane. Center: Microtubules (green) transport cargo. Rear: Actin-myosin contraction (blue) retracts tail. IFs (yellow) provide integrity.

cycle.operation: Protrusion (actin polymerization) → Adhesion (integrin clustering) → Translocation (myosin contraction) → Retraction (rear release)
cycle.purpose: Enable directed cell movement for development, immunity, and wound healing

image.alt: Four stages of cell migration
image.caption: 1. Protrusion: Actin pushes front. 2. Adhesion: Focal adhesions form. 3. Translocation: Cell body moves forward. 4. Retraction: Rear detaches. Cycle repeats.

cycle.operation: Prophase (spindle assembly) → Metaphase (chromosome alignment) → Anaphase (segregation) → Telophase (cytokinesis)
cycle.purpose: Accurately distribute genetic material to daughter cells

image.alt: Cytoskeletal changes during mitosis
image.caption: Prophase: Microtubules form spindle. Metaphase: Chromosomes align. Anaphase: Microtubules pull chromosomes. Telophase: Actin ring divides cell.

sections.introduction: Cell migration is a cyclic process requiring coordinated cytoskeletal remodeling: actin polymerization extends the leading edge, adhesions anchor the protrusion, myosin contraction pulls the cell body forward, and rear adhesions release. This cycle is regulated by Rho GTPases responding to chemotactic gradients.

process_flow.process: Protrusion: Actin Polymerization at Leading Edge
process_flow.description: Cell extends lamellipodia (broad) or filopodia (narrow) via actin polymerization pushing plasma membrane forward.
process_flow.mechanism: Rac/Cdc42 activate WAVE/WASP, which activate Arp2/3 complex. Arp2/3 nucleates branched actin network. Formins nucleate linear bundles (filopodia). ATP-actin adds to plus ends oriented toward membrane. Membrane resistance balanced by polymerization force.
process_flow.result: Leading edge extends forward; new membrane area added via exocytosis

image.alt: Actin polymerization driving membrane protrusion
image.caption: Arp2/3 (blue) nucleates branched actin (red). Plus ends push membrane (yellow). Rac/Cdc42 signaling activates nucleators. Creates lamellipodia/filopodia.

process_flow.process: Adhesion: Integrin Clustering and Focal Complex Formation
process_flow.description: Protrusion forms transient adhesions (focal complexes) that mature into focal adhesions, anchoring actin to extracellular matrix.
process_flow.mechanism: Integrins bind ECM ligands (fibronectin, collagen), cluster, and recruit talin, vinculin, paxillin. Actin stress fibers connect to adhesions via α-actinin. RhoA signaling promotes maturation. Adhesions transmit contractile force to substrate.
process_flow.result: Cell anchored to substrate; force transmission enabled; signaling hub formed

image.alt: Focal adhesion structure linking actin to ECM
image.caption: Integrins (red) bind ECM. Talin/vinculin (blue) link to actin (green). RhoA promotes maturation. Transmits contractile force for traction.

process_flow.description: Actin-myosin contraction pulls cell body forward toward leading edge adhesions.
process_flow.mechanism: Myosin II filaments slide actin stress fibers, generating contractile force. RhoA activates ROCK, phosphorylating myosin light chain (MLC), increasing myosin ATPase activity. Contraction pulls centrosome and nucleus forward. Microtubules stabilize direction by targeting adhesions for disassembly at rear.
process_flow.result: Cell body moves forward; tension maintained across cell

image.alt: Myosin II contraction pulling cell body forward
image.caption: Myosin II (blue) contracts actin stress fibers (red). Pulls cell body toward front adhesions. RhoA/ROCK signaling activates myosin.

process_flow.process: Retraction: Rear Adhesion Disassembly and Tail Release
process_flow.description: Adhesions at cell rear disassemble, allowing tail to detach and complete migration cycle.
process_flow.mechanism: Microtubules target rear adhesions, delivering disassembly factors (e.g., calpain, FAK). RhoA activity decreases at rear, reducing contractility. Membrane blebbing may assist detachment. Rear retracts via actin-myosin contraction. Components recycled to front for new protrusion.
process_flow.result: Cell tail releases; migration cycle complete; components recycled

image.alt: Rear adhesion disassembly and tail retraction
image.caption: Microtubules (green) target rear adhesions (red) for disassembly. Tail retracts. Components recycled to front. Completes migration cycle.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Cilia, Flagella, and Muscle Contraction
sections.definition: Specialized cytoskeletal structures (cilia, flagella, sarcomeres) utilize conserved motor-filament mechanisms for specific functions: fluid movement, cell propulsion, and tissue contraction.

process_types.energy_profile: ATP-dependent (dynein ATPase); continuous energy consumption for beating
process_types.direction: Bidirectional bending via sliding microtubule doublets
process_types.function: Move fluid over cell surface (cilia) or propel cell through fluid (flagella); sensory functions (primary cilia)
goals: Transport mucus, eggs, or cerebrospinal fluid
Sense mechanical and chemical signals (primary cilia)
process_types.energy_source: ATP hydrolysis by axonemal dynein arms

primary_example.process: Axoneme Structure and Sliding Filament Mechanism
primary_example.explanation: Cilia/flagella contain axoneme: 9 outer microtubule doublets surrounding 2 central singlets (9+2 structure). Dynein arms on one doublet walk toward minus end of adjacent doublet. Since doublets are anchored at base and cross-linked by nexin, sliding is converted to bending. Radial spokes regulate dynein activity for coordinated beating. Defects cause primary ciliary dyskinesia (Kartagener syndrome): chronic respiratory infections, infertility, situs inversus.

image.alt: Cross-section and longitudinal view of ciliary axoneme
image.caption: 9+2 arrangement: 9 doublets (green) + 2 central (blue). Dynein arms (red) slide doublets. Nexin links (yellow) convert sliding to bending. Radial spokes regulate.

examples.explanation: Non-motile primary cilia lack central pair (9+0). Function as cellular antennae sensing flow, light, odorants, and growth factors. Polycystin channels in cilia detect fluid flow in kidney tubules. Defects cause polycystic kidney disease (PKD): uncontrolled cell proliferation and cyst formation due to lost flow sensing.

image.alt: Primary cilium sensing fluid flow in kidney tubule
image.caption: Primary cilium (green) bends with flow. Polycystin channels (red) open, Ca²⁺ influx signals cell. Defects cause cysts. Sensory role distinct from motile cilia.

image.alt: Comparison of motile and primary cilia structures
image.caption: Motile cilia (9+2): Dynein arms for movement. Primary cilia (9+0): No dynein, sensory receptors. Both use IFT for assembly. Critical for development and homeostasis.

process_types.energy_profile: ATP-dependent (myosin ATPase); high energy consumption during sustained contraction
process_types.direction: Unidirectional sliding of actin toward sarcomere center
process_types.function: Generate force for body movement, heart pumping, and peristalsis
goals: Convert chemical energy (ATP) into mechanical work
Generate graded force via motor unit recruitment
Enable rapid movement via fast-twitch fibers
process_types.energy_source: ATP hydrolysis by myosin II heads; creatine phosphate buffer

primary_example.explanation: Action potential travels down T-tubules, activating dihydropyridine receptors (DHPR). DHPR mechanically opens ryanodine receptors (RyR) on sarcoplasmic reticulum, releasing Ca²⁺. Ca²⁺ binds troponin C, moving tropomyosin to expose myosin-binding sites on actin. Myosin heads bind, power stroke, detach (ATP binding), reset (ATP hydrolysis). Ca²⁺ pumped back by SERCA for relaxation.

image.alt: Pathway from action potential to muscle contraction
image.caption: AP → T-tubule → DHPR → RyR → Ca²⁺ release → Troponin binding → Tropomyosin shift → Myosin binding → Contraction. SERCA pumps Ca²⁺ back for relaxation.

image.alt: Sarcomere structure before and after contraction
image.caption: Relaxed: I-band and H-zone wide. Contracted: Actin slides over myosin, I-band and H-zone narrow. Z-discs move closer. Sarcomere shortens, muscle contracts.

sections.relationship: Cilia/flagella and muscle both use motor-filament sliding but differ in organization: cilia use microtubule-dynein for bending; muscle uses actin-myosin for linear shortening. Both require precise Ca²⁺ regulation and ATP supply. Defects in either cause severe diseases (ciliopathies, muscular dystrophies), highlighting cytoskeletal importance.

sections.section_title: Molecular Mechanisms: Regulation by Rho GTPases
sections.purpose: Cytoskeletal dynamics are spatially and temporally regulated by Rho family GTPases (Rho, Rac, Cdc42), which act as molecular switches coordinating actin polymerization, myosin contraction, and microtubule stabilization in response to extracellular signals.

activation.definition: Rho GTPases cycle between active GTP-bound and inactive GDP-bound states, regulated by GEFs (activate), GAPs (inactivate), and GDIs (sequester).
activation.effect: Active GTPases bind effectors (WAVE, WASP, formins, ROCK) to remodel cytoskeleton. Rac promotes lamellipodia; Cdc42 promotes filopodia; Rho promotes stress fibers and contractility. Spatiotemporal activation patterns determine cell shape and movement direction.

image.alt: Rho GTPase activation cycle and downstream effects
image.caption: GDP-bound (inactive) → GEF activates → GTP-bound (active) → binds effectors → cytoskeletal remodeling → GAP inactivates → GDP-bound. GDIs sequester in cytosol.

key_properties.property: Rac1: Lamellipodia Formation and Cell Spreading
key_properties.explanation: Rac1 activates WAVE complex, which activates Arp2/3 to nucleate branched actin networks at cell periphery. This creates lamellipodia for cell spreading and migration. Rac1 also inhibits RhoA via p190RhoGAP, ensuring protrusion dominates over contraction at leading edge. Constitutive activation causes excessive spreading; inhibition prevents migration.

image.alt: Rac1 signaling pathway leading to lamellipodia formation
image.caption: Rac1-GTP → WAVE → Arp2/3 → branched actin → lamellipodia. Inhibits RhoA at front. Essential for migration and phagocytosis.

key_properties.property: Cdc42: Filopodia Formation and Cell Polarity
key_properties.explanation: Cdc42 activates WASP and formins (mDia2), nucleating linear actin bundles for filopodia. Filopodia sense environment and establish cell polarity. Cdc42 also activates PAR complex (PAR3/PAR6/aPKC) for apical-basal polarity in epithelia. Critical for neuronal growth cone guidance and immune synapse formation.

image.alt: Cdc42 signaling pathway leading to filopodia formation
image.caption: Cdc42-GTP → WASP/Formins → linear actin bundles → filopodia. Activates PAR complex for polarity. Sensory and guidance functions.

key_properties.property: RhoA: Stress Fiber Assembly and Contractility
key_properties.explanation: RhoA activates formin (mDia1) for linear actin nucleation and ROCK (Rho kinase). ROCK phosphorylates myosin light chain (MLC) and inhibits MLC phosphatase, increasing myosin II activity. This promotes stress fiber assembly and contractility. Dominant at cell rear and sides during migration. Overactivation causes excessive tension and reduced migration.

image.alt: RhoA signaling pathway leading to stress fiber formation
image.caption: RhoA-GTP → mDia1 (actin) + ROCK (myosin) → stress fibers + contractility. Dominant at rear/sides. Balances Rac/Cdc42 at front for directed migration.

summary: The cytoskeleton is a dynamic, energy-dependent network of three filament systems—microfilaments (actin), intermediate filaments, and microtubules—that maintains cell shape, enables motility, facilitates intracellular transport, and drives cell division. Microfilaments generate force for cell crawling and muscle contraction via actin polymerization and myosin motors. Intermediate filaments provide tensile strength and tissue integrity through tissue-specific proteins (keratins, vimentin, lamins). Microtubules serve as tracks for kinesin and dynein motors, organize the mitotic spindle, and form cilia/flagella axonemes. All systems exhibit polarity and nucleotide-dependent dynamics (treadmilling, dynamic instability) regulated by accessory proteins and Rho GTPase signaling pathways. Motor proteins convert ATP hydrolysis into mechanical work for cargo transport and filament sliding. Coordinated cytoskeletal remodeling enables cell migration (protrusion, adhesion, contraction, retraction), cytokinesis, and specialized functions like ciliary beating and muscle contraction. Defects cause diverse diseases including neurodegeneration, ciliopathies, muscular dystrophies, and cancer metastasis, underscoring the cytoskeleton's essential role in cellular and organismal homeostasis.
